"Treasury Building (Washington, D.C.)") 1500 [Pennsylvania Avenue](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue "Pennsylvania Avenue"), [NW](/wiki/Northwest_(Washington,_D.C.) "Northwest (Washington, D.C.)") [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), U.S [38°53′51″N 77°02′04″W﻿ / ﻿38.8975°N 77.0344°W﻿ / 38.8975; -77.0344](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&params=38.8975_N_77.0344_W_type:landmark_region:US-DC)

* [Scott Bessent](/wiki/Scott_Bessent "Scott Bessent"), [Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") * [Derek Theurer](/wiki/Derek_Theurer "Derek Theurer"), Acting [Deputy Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Deputy_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Deputy Secretary of the Treasury") * [Brandon Beach](/wiki/Brandon_Beach "Brandon Beach"), [Treasurer](/wiki/Treasurer_of_the_United_States "Treasurer of the United States")

* [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") * [United States Mint](/wiki/United_States_Mint "United States Mint") * [Bureau of Engraving and Printing](/wiki/Bureau_of_Engraving_and_Printing "Bureau of Engraving and Printing") * [Several others](/wiki/Category:United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury_agencies "Category:United States Department of the Treasury agencies")

(**USDT**)[[2]](#cite_note-isbn0313245789-pp275-2) is the [national treasury](/wiki/Treasury "Treasury") and finance department of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It is one of 15 current [U.S. government departments](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The treasury executes [currency circulation](/wiki/Currency_in_circulation "Currency in circulation") in the domestic fiscal system, [collects](/wiki/Tax_collector "Tax collector") all [federal taxes](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States") through the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service"), manages [U.S. government debt](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security"), [licenses and supervises banks](/wiki/Bank_regulation#Licensing_and_supervision "Bank regulation") and [thrift institutions](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association"), and advises the [legislative](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States#Legislative_branch "Federal government of the United States") and [executive branches](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States#Executive_branch "Federal government of the United States") on [fiscal policy](/wiki/Fiscal_policy_of_the_United_States "Fiscal policy of the United States").

The **Department of the Treasury** (**USDT**)[[2]](#cite_note-isbn0313245789-pp275-2) is the [national treasury](/wiki/Treasury "Treasury") and finance department of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It is one of 15 current [U.S. government departments](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The treasury executes [currency circulation](/wiki/Currency_in_circulation "Currency in circulation") in the domestic fiscal system, [collects](/wiki/Tax_collector "Tax collector") all [federal taxes](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States") through the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service"), manages [U.S. government debt](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security"), [licenses and supervises banks](/wiki/Bank_regulation#Licensing_and_supervision "Bank regulation") and [thrift institutions](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association"), and advises the [legislative](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States#Legislative_branch "Federal government of the United States") and [executive branches](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States#Executive_branch "Federal government of the United States") on [fiscal policy](/wiki/Fiscal_policy_of_the_United_States "Fiscal policy of the United States").

The department is administered by the [secretary of the treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury"), who is a member of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States"). The department oversees the [Bureau of Engraving and Printing](/wiki/Bureau_of_Engraving_and_Printing "Bureau of Engraving and Printing") and the [U.S. Mint](/wiki/United_States_Mint "United States Mint"), two federal agencies responsible for printing all [paper currency](/wiki/Paper_currency "Paper currency") and minting [coins](/wiki/United_States_coinage "United States coinage"). The [treasurer of the United States](/wiki/Treasurer_of_the_United_States "Treasurer of the United States") has limited statutory duties, but advises the Secretary on various matters such as coinage and currency production.[[4]](#cite_note-ustreasurydepttreasurer2018-4) Both the treasurer and treasury secretary's signatures appear on all [U.S. dollar bank notes](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Note "Federal Reserve Note").[[5]](#cite_note-crutsinger2017-5)

The department was established by an [act of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress") in 1789 to manage government [revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue").[[6]](#cite_note-Act-to-establish-6) The first secretary of the treasury was [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") from [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), a [Founding Father](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"). Appointed by [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), the nation's first [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), Hamilton was sworn into office on September 11, 1789.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Hamilton established the nation's early financial system and for several years was a major presence in [Washington's administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_George_Washington "Presidency of George Washington").[[8]](#cite_note-wolffscanlan2006-8) The department is customarily referred to as "Treasury", solely, without any [preceding article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") – a transitional remnant from [British](/wiki/British_English "British English") to [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

The history of the Department of the Treasury began during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") when the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress"), convening in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), deliberated the crucial question of how it would finance the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") in which the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") ultimately sought [independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") from the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), then led by [King George III](/wiki/George_III "George III").[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

The Congress had no power to [levy](/wiki/Tax_levies "Tax levies") and [collect](/wiki/Tax_collector "Tax collector") taxes, nor was there a tangible basis for securing funds from foreign investors or governments. The delegates resolved to issue paper money in the form of [bills of credit](/wiki/Bills_of_credit "Bills of credit"), promising [redemption](/wiki/Fiat_money "Fiat money") in [coin](/wiki/Coin "Coin") on faith in the revolutionary cause.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) On June 22, 1775, only a few days after the [Battle of Bunker Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_Bunker_Hill "Battle of Bunker Hill"), the Continental Congress issued $2 million in bills; on July 25, 28 citizens of Philadelphia were employed by Congress to sign and number the currency.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On July 29, 1775, the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") assigned responsibility for the administration of the revolutionary government's finances to joint Continental treasurers [George Clymer](/wiki/George_Clymer "George Clymer") and [Michael Hillegas](/wiki/Michael_Hillegas "Michael Hillegas").[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) Congress stipulated that each of the colonies contribute to the Continental government's funds. To ensure proper and efficient handling of the growing [national debt](/wiki/National_debt "National debt") in the face of weak economic and political ties between the colonies, the Congress, on February 17, 1776, designated a committee of five to superintend the treasury, [settle accounts](/wiki/Settlement_(finance) "Settlement (finance)"), and report periodically to the Congress.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) On April 1, a Treasury Office of Accounts, consisting of an auditor general and [clerks](/wiki/Clerk "Clerk"), was established to facilitate the settlement of claims and to keep the public accounts for the government of the United Colonies. With the signing of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") on July 4, 1776, the newborn republic as a [sovereign nation](/wiki/Sovereign_nation "Sovereign nation") was able to secure loans from abroad.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Despite the infusion of foreign and domestic loans, the [united colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") were unable to establish a well-organized agency for financial administration.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) Michael Hillegas was first called Treasurer of the United States on May 14, 1777. The Treasury Office was reorganized three times between 1778 and 1781. The $241.5 million in paper Continental bills [devalued](/wiki/Devaluation "Devaluation") rapidly. By May 1781, the dollar [collapsed](/wiki/Hyperinflation "Hyperinflation") at a rate of from 500 to 1000 to 1 against [hard currency](/wiki/Hard_currency "Hard currency").[[12]](#cite_note-:03-12) Protests against the worthless money swept the colonies, giving rise to the expression "[not worth a Continental](/wiki/Early_American_currency#Continental_currency "Early American currency")".[[13]](#cite_note-:02-13)

### Late 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Late 18th century")]

Since the late 18th century, the office has been [customarily referred](/wiki/British_English "British English") to as the singular "Treasury", without any [preceding article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)"), as a remnant of the country's transition from [British](/wiki/British_English "British English") to [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) For example, the department notes its guiding purpose as "Treasury's mission" instead of "the Treasury's mission."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Robert Morris](/wiki/Robert_Morris_(financier) "Robert Morris (financier)") was designated Superintendent of Finance in 1781 and restored stability to the nation's finances. Morris, a wealthy colonial [merchant](/wiki/Merchant "Merchant"), was nicknamed "the financier" because of his reputation for procuring funds or goods on a moment's notice. His staff included a [comptroller](/wiki/Comptroller "Comptroller"), a [treasurer](/wiki/Treasurer "Treasurer"), a [register](/wiki/Register_of_the_Treasury "Register of the Treasury"), and [auditors](/wiki/Auditor "Auditor"), who managed the country's finances through 1784, when Morris resigned because of ill health. The treasury board, consisting of three commissioners, continued to oversee the finances of the confederation of former colonies until September 1789.

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Founding")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/71/Seal_of_the_United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury_%281789-1968%29.png)](/wiki/File:Seal_of_the_United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury_(1789-1968).png)

Original seal, dating from before 1968

The [First United States Congress](/wiki/1st_United_States_Congress "1st United States Congress") convened in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") on March 4, 1789, marking the beginning of government under the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"). On September 2, 1789, Congress created a permanent institution for the management of government finances:

> Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That there shall be a Department of Treasury, in which shall be the following officers, namely: a Secretary of the Treasury, to be deemed head of the department; a Comptroller, an Auditor, a Treasurer, a Register, and an [Assistant to the Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Assistant_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Assistant Secretary of the Treasury"), which assistant shall be appointed by the said Secretary.[[6]](#cite_note-Act-to-establish-6)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") took the oath of office as the first [secretary of the treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") on September 11, 1789. Hamilton had served as [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington")'s *[aide-de-camp](/wiki/Aide-de-camp "Aide-de-camp")* during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and was influential in the [ratification](/wiki/United_States_Constitution#1788_ratification "United States Constitution") of the Constitution. Hamilton's [financial and managerial acumen](/wiki/Business_acumen "Business acumen") made him a logical choice for addressing the problem of the new nation's heavy [war debt](/wiki/War_debt "War debt"). Hamilton was recommended to Washington by [Robert Morris](/wiki/Robert_Morris_(financier) "Robert Morris (financier)") from [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), who was also a Second Continental Congress delegate. Morris was Washington's first choice for the position, but declined the appointment.[[18]](#cite_note-adams2018-18) His first official act as secretary was to submit a report to Congress in which he laid the foundation for the nation's financial health. Hamilton's portrait appears on the [obverse](/wiki/Obverse_and_reverse "Obverse and reverse") of the [ten-dollar bill](/wiki/United_States_ten-dollar_bill "United States ten-dollar bill"), while the [Treasury Building](/wiki/Treasury_Building_(Washington,_D.C.) "Treasury Building (Washington, D.C.)") in Washington, D.C. is depicted on the [reverse](/wiki/Obverse_and_reverse "Obverse and reverse").[[19]](#cite_note-uscep2018$10-19)

To the surprise of many legislators, he insisted upon [federal assumption](/wiki/Debt_Assumption "Debt Assumption") and dollar-for-dollar repayment of the country's $75 million debt in order to revitalize the [public credit](/wiki/Public_credit "Public credit"): "[T]he debt of the United States was the price of liberty. The faith of America has been repeatedly pledged for it, and with solemnities that give peculiar force to the obligation."[[20]](#cite_note-syrett1962vol6-20) Hamilton foresaw the development of industry and trade in the United States, suggesting that government revenues be based upon [customs duties](/wiki/Customs_duties "Customs duties").[[20]](#cite_note-syrett1962vol6-20) His sound [financial policies](/wiki/Fiscal_policy "Fiscal policy") also inspired investment in the [Bank of the United States](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States"), which acted as the government's [fiscal agent](/wiki/Fiscal_agent "Fiscal agent").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Department of Treasury believes their seal was created by [Francis Hopkinson](/wiki/Francis_Hopkinson#Great_Seal_of_the_United_States "Francis Hopkinson"), the treasurer of loans. He submitted bills to Congress in 1780 that authorized the design of department seals, including a seal for the Board of Treasury. While it is not certain that Hopkinson designed the seal, it closely resembles others he created.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### 19th and 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th and 20th centuries")]

In 1861, Sophia Holmes became the first Black woman to be employed by the Treasury Department and by the [Federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") when [Senator Henry Wilson](/wiki/Henry_Wilson "Henry Wilson"), [James G. Blaine](/wiki/James_G._Blaine "James G. Blaine") and others advocated for her hiring as a janitor under Secretary of the Treasury [Francis Spinner](/wiki/Francis_E._Spinner "Francis E. Spinner"). She was paid fifteen dollars per month. In 1862, she prevented a major theft from the department of more than $200,000 when she came across a box filled with U.S. currency, including a number of thousand-dollar bills, and reported it to Secretary Spinner.

U.S. president [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") subsequently honored her with a commendation for her actions, and the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") rewarded her with an appointment for life as a messenger with its department of Issues.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Another early woman hired by the department was [Jennie Douglas](/wiki/Jennie_Douglas "Jennie Douglas") in 1862.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Douglas, who was also recruited by Spinner, is sometimes attributed to having been the first woman to have held an appointed position in the federal government.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

#### Departmental reorganization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Departmental reorganization")]

Further information: [Homeland Security Act](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Act "Homeland Security Act")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Treasury_departement.jpg/250px-Treasury_departement.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treasury_departement.jpg)

The [Treasury Building](/wiki/Treasury_Building_(Washington,_D.C.) "Treasury Building (Washington, D.C.)") at 1500 [Pennsylvania Avenue](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue "Pennsylvania Avenue"), [NW](/wiki/Northwest,_Washington,_D.C. "Northwest, Washington, D.C.") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

The [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") transferred several agencies that had previously been under the aegis of the Treasury Department to other departments as a consequence of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"). Effective January 24, 2003, the [Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms](/wiki/Bureau_of_Alcohol,_Tobacco_and_Firearms "Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms") (ATF), which had been a bureau of the department since 1972, was extensively reorganized under the provisions of the [Homeland Security Act of 2002](/wiki/Homeland_Security_Act "Homeland Security Act"). The [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency") functions of ATF, including the regulation of legitimate traffic in [firearms](/wiki/Firearms "Firearms") and [explosives](/wiki/Explosives "Explosives"), were transferred to the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") as the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms, and Explosives (BATFE).[[27]](#cite_note-Washington_Post_2003-27) The regulatory and tax collection functions of ATF related to legitimate traffic in alcohol and tobacco remained with the treasury at its new [Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau](/wiki/Alcohol_and_Tobacco_Tax_and_Trade_Bureau "Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau") (TTB).[[28]](#cite_note-TTBGov_2015-28)

Effective March 1, 2003, the [Federal Law Enforcement Training Center](/wiki/Federal_Law_Enforcement_Training_Center "Federal Law Enforcement Training Center"), the [U.S. Customs Service](/wiki/United_States_Customs_Service "United States Customs Service"), and the [U.S. Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service") were transferred to the newly created [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security").[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the 2015 [Center for Effective Government](/wiki/Center_for_Effective_Government "Center for Effective Government") analysis of the 15 federal agencies that receive the most [Freedom of Information Act](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information_Act_(United_States) "Freedom of Information Act (United States)") (FOIA) requests (using 2012 and 2013 data, the most recent years available), Treasury failed to earn a satisfactory overall grade.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### 2020 data breach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020 data breach")]

Main article: [2020 United States Treasury and Department of Commerce data breach](/wiki/2020_United_States_Treasury_and_Department_of_Commerce_data_breach "2020 United States Treasury and Department of Commerce data breach")

In 2020, Treasury [suffered a data breach](/wiki/2020_United_States_Treasury_and_Department_of_Commerce_data_breach "2020 United States Treasury and Department of Commerce data breach") following a [cyberattack](/wiki/Cyberattack "Cyberattack") likely conducted by a [nation state adversary](/wiki/Advanced_persistent_threat "Advanced persistent threat"), possibly Russia.[[31]](#cite_note-wapo-31)[[32]](#cite_note-reuters-32) This was in fact the first detected case of the much wider [2020 United States federal government data breach](/wiki/2020_United_States_federal_government_data_breach "2020 United States federal government data breach"), which involved at least eight federal departments.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Basic functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Basic functions")]

The basic functions of the Department of the Treasury include:[[34]](#cite_note-34)

* Producing all currency and coinage of the U.S.;
* Collecting [taxes, duties](/wiki/Tax,_tariff_and_trade "Tax, tariff and trade") and money paid to and due to the U.S.;
* Paying all bills of the U.S.;
* Managing the federal finances;
* Managing government accounts (including the [Treasury General Account](/wiki/Treasury_General_Account "Treasury General Account")) and the [United States public debt](/wiki/United_States_public_debt "United States public debt");
* Supervising national banks and [thrift institutions](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking");
* Advising on domestic and international financial, [monetary](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy"), [economic](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy"), [trade and tax policy](/wiki/Industrial_policy "Industrial policy") ([fiscal policy](/wiki/Fiscal_policy "Fiscal policy") being the sum of these);
* Enforcing federal finance and tax laws;
* Investigating and prosecuting [tax evaders](/wiki/Tax_evasion "Tax evasion");
* Publishing statistical reports

With respect to the estimation of revenues for the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), Treasury serves a purpose parallel to that of the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget") for the estimation of spending for the executive branch, the [Joint Committee on Taxation](/wiki/United_States_Congress_Joint_Committee_on_Taxation "United States Congress Joint Committee on Taxation") for the estimation of revenues for Congress, and the [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office") for the estimation of spending for Congress.

From 1830 until 1901, responsibility for overseeing [weights and measures](/wiki/Weights_and_measures "Weights and measures") was carried out by the Office of Standard Weights and Measures under the auspices of the Treasury Department.[[35]](#cite_note-archives-35) After 1901, that responsibility was assigned to the agency that subsequently became known as the [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology").

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Organization")]

The Department of the Treasury is organized into two major components: the departmental offices and the operating bureaus. The departmental offices are primarily responsible for the formulation of policy and management of the department as a whole, while the operating bureaus carry out the specific operations assigned to the department.

### Bureaus

| [Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau](/wiki/Alcohol_and_Tobacco_Tax_and_Trade_Bureau "Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau") (TTB) | The Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau (TTB) is responsible for enforcing and administering laws covering the production, use, and distribution of alcohol and tobacco products. TTB also collects excise taxes for firearms and ammunition. |
| [Bureau of Engraving and Printing](/wiki/Bureau_of_Engraving_and_Printing "Bureau of Engraving and Printing") (BEP) | The Bureau of Engraving and Printing (BEP) designs and manufactures U.S. currency, securities, and other official certificates and awards. |
| [Bureau of the Fiscal Service](/wiki/Bureau_of_the_Fiscal_Service "Bureau of the Fiscal Service") | The Bureau of the Fiscal Service was formed from the consolidation of the Financial Management Service and the Bureau of the Public Debt. Its mission is to promote the financial integrity and operational efficiency of the U.S. government through exceptional accounting, financing, collections, payments, and shared services. |
| [Community Development Financial Institution](/wiki/Community_Development_Financial_Institutions_Fund "Community Development Financial Institutions Fund") (CDFI) Fund | The Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) Fund was created to expand the availability of credit, investment capital, and financial services in distressed urban and rural communities. |
| [Financial Crimes Enforcement Network](/wiki/Financial_Crimes_Enforcement_Network "Financial Crimes Enforcement Network") (FinCEN) | The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) supports law enforcement investigative efforts and fosters interagency and global cooperation against domestic and international financial crimes. It also provides U.S. policymakers with strategic analyses of domestic and worldwide trends and patterns. |
| [Inspector General](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_the_Treasury,_Office_of_Inspector_General "U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Inspector General") | The Inspector General conducts independent audits, investigations and reviews to help the Treasury Department accomplish its mission; improve its programs and operations; promote economy, efficiency and effectiveness; and prevent and detect fraud and abuse. |
| [Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration](/wiki/Treasury_Inspector_General_for_Tax_Administration "Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration") (TIGTA) | The Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration (TIGTA) provides leadership and coordination and recommends policy for activities designed to promote economy, efficiency, and effectiveness in the administration of the internal revenue laws. TIGTA also recommends policies to prevent and detect fraud and abuse in the programs and operations of the IRS and related entities. |
| [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") | The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) is the largest of Treasury's bureaus. It is responsible for determining, assessing, and collecting internal revenue in the United States. |
| [Office of the Comptroller of the Currency](/wiki/Office_of_the_Comptroller_of_the_Currency "Office of the Comptroller of the Currency") (OCC) | The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) charters, regulates, and supervises national banks to ensure a safe, sound, and competitive banking system that supports the citizens, communities, and economy of the United States. |
| [U.S. Mint](/wiki/United_States_Mint "United States Mint") | The U.S. Mint designs and manufactures domestic, bullion and foreign coins as well as commemorative medals and other numismatic items. The Mint also distributes U.S. coins to the Federal Reserve banks as well as maintains physical custody and protection of the nation's silver and gold assets. |
